claims.claim_text: The module opens with a specific January 2013 Google scene claiming Mikolov was simplifying speech-recognition models down to one hidden layer.
claims.span_hint: section: The Accidental Discovery That Changed AI Forever / lines 19-21
claims.anchor_text: Tomáš Mikolov was frustrated. The Czech-born researcher had spent months trying to make Google's speech recognition faster. His approach was simple: strip down the neural network architecture to its absolute minimum—just one hidden layer instead of the complex deep networks everyone else was using.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In 2013, Tomas Mikolov and collaborators at Google published influential work on learning efficient word vectors with comparatively simple neural architectures.
claims.rationale: The named date/location/motivation scene is a detailed anecdote without a primary source directly backing those specifics.

claims.claim_text: The module attributes an unsourced direct quote to Mikolov's Google colleagues about simple models not working.
claims.span_hint: section: The Accidental Discovery That Changed AI Forever / line 23
claims.anchor_text: His colleagues at Google were skeptical. "You can't get good results with such a simple model," they said.
claims.suggested_rewrite: At the time, the result was striking because it showed that relatively simple architectures could learn useful word representations efficiently.
claims.rationale: This is a verbatim historical quote with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The paper is claimed to have over 40,000 citations and to power everything from Google Search to ChatGPT.
claims.span_hint: section: The Accidental Discovery That Changed AI Forever / line 35
claims.anchor_text: He published his findings in a paper called "Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space." It has since been cited over 40,000 times, making it one of the most influential machine learning papers ever written. The technique he accidentally discovered—word embeddings—became the foundation of modern NLP, powering everything from Google Search to ChatGPT.
claims.suggested_rewrite: He published early word-vector results in the 2013 paper "Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space," and that line of work became highly influential in later NLP systems.
claims.rationale: The dynamic citation count and the sweeping product-impact claim are not directly supported by a single authoritative source.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI text-embedding-3-small uses 1536 dimensions.
claims.span_hint: section: What Are Embeddings? / dimensionality table row 1
claims.rationale: OpenAI's embeddings guide states that text-embedding-3-small defaults to 1536 dimensions.

claims.claim_text: OpenAI text-embedding-3-large uses 3072 dimensions.
claims.span_hint: section: What Are Embeddings? / dimensionality table row 2
claims.rationale: OpenAI's embeddings guide states that text-embedding-3-large defaults to 3072 dimensions.

claims.claim_text: The table presents Anthropic as a first-party embeddings provider with a 1024-dimensional Voyage offering.
claims.span_hint: section: What Are Embeddings? / dimensionality table row 3
claims.anchor_text: | **Anthropic (Voyage)** | 1024 | Optimized for retrieval |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Voyage AI** | 1024 | Retrieval-focused embedding models |
claims.rationale: The current wording conflates Anthropic with Voyage as if Anthropic offered its own embeddings model.

claims.claim_text: The table assigns Sentence-BERT a universal 384-768 dimension range.
claims.span_hint: section: What Are Embeddings? / dimensionality table row 4
claims.anchor_text: | **Sentence-BERT** | 384-768 | Open-source, local deployment |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Sentence-transformer models** | Commonly a few hundred to around a thousand dimensions | Open-source, local deployment |
claims.rationale: The exact range is model-family dependent and is not directly backed by one authoritative source for all Sentence-BERT variants.

claims.claim_text: The table assigns Word2Vec a universal 100-300 dimension range.
claims.span_hint: section: What Are Embeddings? / dimensionality table row 5
claims.anchor_text: | **Word2Vec** | 100-300 | Historical, word-level only |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Word2Vec** | Commonly a few hundred dimensions | Historical, word-level only |
claims.rationale: The exact dimensionality depends on the training setup and should not be presented as a fixed universal range without a specific source.

claims.claim_text: The module states that embedding models are trained using contrastive learning as a universal rule.
claims.span_hint: section: How Embeddings Work / line 176
claims.anchor_text: Embedding models are trained using **contrastive learning**:
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many modern retrieval and sentence-embedding models are trained with contrastive objectives:
claims.rationale: Contrastive training is common, but the unqualified universal claim overstates the training method across all embedding models.

claims.claim_text: The module claims models generate millions of positive/negative pairs from web data during training.
claims.span_hint: section: How Embeddings Work / training process item 1
claims.anchor_text: 1. Generate millions of (positive, negative) pairs from web data
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. Generate large numbers of training pairs from available data
claims.rationale: The exact scale and data source are model-specific and unsourced here.

claims.claim_text: The module describes a 2023 Google discovery that embeddings separated sarcasm from genuine statements.
claims.span_hint: section: How Embeddings Work / Did You Know box
claims.anchor_text: The contrastive learning approach has become so effective that modern embedding models can understand nuances that even humans sometimes miss. In 2023, researchers at Google discovered that their embedding model had learned to distinguish between different types of irony—sarcastic statements clustered separately from genuine statements, even when the words were nearly identical. The model had learned the "shape" of irony from patterns in billions of text examples. This emergent ability wasn't explicitly programmed; it arose naturally from the training process, suggesting that meaning has a geometric structure that neural networks can discover.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Modern embedding models can capture subtle semantic distinctions, but this module should not attribute a specific 2023 Google irony-discovery story without a source.
claims.rationale: I could not verify this specific named research anecdote from an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: The module claims OpenAI text-embedding-3 models were trained on hundreds of billions of tokens and cost millions of dollars to train.
claims.span_hint: section: How Embeddings Work / The Scale of Training
claims.anchor_text: Modern embedding models are trained on staggering amounts of data. OpenAI's text-embedding-3 models are trained on hundreds of billions of tokens—equivalent to reading every book in the Library of Congress thousands of times. This massive scale is what allows them to understand that "automobile" and "car" are synonyms, or that "bank" has different meanings in financial and river contexts. The training compute for these models can cost millions of dollars, but the resulting embeddings are available to anyone for fractions of a cent per thousand tokens.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Modern embedding models are trained on very large corpora, and current API pricing makes inference inexpensive for many applications.
claims.rationale: OpenAI does not publicly document those exact training-token and training-cost figures for text-embedding-3 models.

claims.claim_text: The module says Anthropic partners with Voyage AI for embeddings.
claims.span_hint: section: Generating Embeddings / Option 2 opening line
claims.anchor_text: Anthropic partners with Voyage AI for embeddings:
claims.suggested_rewrite: Anthropic's embeddings guidance points readers to Voyage AI as one example provider:
claims.rationale: The current phrasing makes a stronger partnership claim than the sourceable public documentation supports.

claims.claim_text: Voyage AI offers voyage-2 with 1024 dimensions optimized for retrieval.
claims.span_hint: section: Generating Embeddings / Voyage Models bullet 1
claims.rationale: Voyage's official docs directly list voyage-2 and its dimensions, but docs.voyageai.com is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Voyage AI offers voyage-large-2 with 1536 dimensions.
claims.span_hint: section: Generating Embeddings / Voyage Models bullet 2
claims.rationale: Voyage's official docs directly list voyage-large-2 and its dimensions, but the docs host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module claims Netflix recommendations are based on cosine similarity over embeddings.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? / Netflix sentence
claims.anchor_text: Cosine similarity is used everywhere in recommender systems! When Netflix recommends movies, it's comparing the embedding of movies you've watched to embeddings of all other movies, then recommending the ones with highest cosine similarity!
claims.suggested_rewrite: Cosine similarity is widely used in retrieval and recommendation systems, but this module should not attribute a specific Netflix production design without a source.
claims.rationale: This asserts a concrete named-company implementation detail without an attributable primary source.

claims.claim_text: The module says Netflix generates more than $1B per year in value from its recommendation system.
claims.span_hint: section: The Netflix Recommendation Engine / numbers bullet 1
claims.anchor_text: - Netflix generates **$1B+ per year** in value from their recommendation system
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Recommendation systems can create large business value for streaming platforms.
claims.rationale: This is a specific company-value claim with a precise dollar figure and no cited source.

claims.claim_text: The module says Netflix replaced its old recommendation system with embeddings in 2019.
claims.span_hint: section: The Netflix Recommendation Engine / numbers bullet 2
claims.anchor_text: - Embeddings replaced their old system in 2019
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Modern recommendation systems often use embedding-based representations.
claims.rationale: This is a dated company-specific architecture claim without a verifiable primary source.

claims.claim_text: The module says 80% of Netflix viewing comes from recommendations.
claims.span_hint: section: The Netflix Recommendation Engine / numbers bullet 3
claims.anchor_text: - 80% of content watched comes from recommendations
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Recommendations account for a large share of viewing on many streaming platforms.
claims.rationale: This is a precise company statistic without a primary source in the module.

claims.claim_text: An anonymized e-commerce case study is said to have search failures costing $50,000 per hour.
claims.span_hint: section: The E-commerce Search Disaster / challenge line
claims.anchor_text: **Challenge**: Holiday season search failures costing $50,000/hour in lost sales
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Challenge**: Holiday-season search failures were materially hurting revenue.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war story with precise financial impact.

claims.claim_text: The case study says search conversion dropped 40% on Black Friday.
claims.span_hint: section: The E-commerce Search Disaster / disaster bullet 1
claims.anchor_text: - Search conversion rate dropped 40% on Black Friday
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Search conversion dropped sharply during peak traffic.
claims.rationale: This is a specific incident metric without any attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The case study says engineers manually mapped 500 synonym pairs by Cyber Monday.
claims.span_hint: section: The E-commerce Search Disaster / disaster bullet 4
claims.anchor_text: - By Cyber Monday, they'd manually mapped 500 synonym pairs—and still missed countless combinations
claims.suggested_rewrite: - The team tried manually maintaining synonym mappings, but the approach did not scale.
claims.rationale: This is a precise operational anecdote without a source.

claims.claim_text: The case study says semantic search increased search conversion by 35%.
claims.span_hint: section: The E-commerce Search Disaster / results bullet 1
claims.anchor_text: - Search conversion rate increased 35%
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Better retrieval can improve search conversion.
claims.rationale: This is a specific improvement figure with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The case study says semantic search added $4.2M in annual revenue.
claims.span_hint: section: The E-commerce Search Disaster / results bullet 4
claims.anchor_text: - Estimated annual revenue increase: $4.2M
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Better retrieval can produce meaningful revenue gains when search is business-critical.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed financial estimate with false precision.

claims.claim_text: A law-firm case study is said to involve 2 million case files.
claims.span_hint: section: The Legal Discovery Breakthrough / challenge line
claims.anchor_text: **Challenge**: Find relevant documents in 2 million case files
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Challenge**: Find relevant documents in a very large legal corpus.
claims.rationale: This is a precise scale claim attached to an unnamed real-world case with no source.

claims.claim_text: The law-firm case study says paralegals billed $150 per hour.
claims.span_hint: section: The Legal Discovery Breakthrough / opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Traditional approach: Teams of paralegals reading documents for weeks, billing $150/hour.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Traditional discovery often relies on substantial human review labor.
claims.rationale: This is a specific pricing claim within an unattributed case study.

claims.claim_text: The law-firm case study says embedding 2 million documents cost about $200 in API calls.
claims.span_hint: section: The Legal Discovery Breakthrough / approach item 1
claims.anchor_text: 1. Embed all 2 million documents (one-time cost: ~$200 in API calls)
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. Embed the document corpus once, then reuse those vectors for retrieval.
claims.rationale: This is a specific cost estimate within an unnamed case study rather than a sourced worked example.

claims.claim_text: The law-firm case study says the old process took 6 weeks, 8 paralegals, and about $180,000.
claims.span_hint: section: The Legal Discovery Breakthrough / numbers bullet 1
claims.anchor_text: - Before: 6 weeks, 8 paralegals, ~$180,000 in billable hours
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Before: the process required substantial time and manual review labor.

claims.suggested_rewrite: - After: the review workflow was dramatically shorter and required much less manual effort.
claims.rationale: This is a precise before/after case-study metric without attribution.

claims.claim_text: The law-firm case study says total savings were about $175,000 per case.

claims.rationale: This is an unattributed financial result with false precision.

claims.claim_text: The module claims self-hosting only makes sense above roughly 100M tokens per month or about $2,000 in API costs.
claims.span_hint: section: When Self-Hosting Makes Sense / break-even paragraph
claims.anchor_text: **Break-even analysis**: At OpenAI's pricing, self-hosting only makes sense above ~100M tokens/month (about $2,000/month in API costs). Below that, the operational overhead of running GPU infrastructure exceeds the API savings.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Break-even analysis**: Whether self-hosting is worth it depends on workload size, latency targets, privacy requirements, and the real operating cost of your infrastructure.
claims.rationale: This exact threshold is highly environment-dependent and is not sourceable as a universal fact.

claims.claim_text: The module says text-embedding-ada-002 cost $0.0001 per 1,000 tokens and was 2000x cheaper than the prior ada model.
claims.span_hint: section: The OpenAI Pricing Shock / after bullet 1
claims.anchor_text: - Price: **$0.0001** per 1,000 tokens (2000x cheaper!)
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Price: dramatically lower than earlier OpenAI embedding offerings of the time.
claims.rationale: The section's before/after comparison is not backed here by a source that directly supports the exact multiplier and comparison baseline used.

claims.claim_text: The module says that by 2024 all major providers offered embeddings below $0.0005 per 1K tokens.
claims.span_hint: section: The OpenAI Pricing Shock / industry reaction bullet 4
claims.anchor_text: - By 2024, all major providers offer embeddings at <$0.0005 per 1K tokens
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Embedding prices fell sharply across the market after major vendors intensified competition.
claims.rationale: This is a dated market-wide pricing claim that requires current multi-vendor evidence and is not directly sourced here.

claims.claim_text: The module attributes a Microsoft Research study saying exact query terms appear in relevant enterprise-search documents only 23% of the time.
claims.span_hint: section: Why Embeddings Beat Keyword Search / opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: The fundamental limitation of keyword search is the "vocabulary mismatch" problem. Users don't use the same words as document authors. A study by Microsoft Research found that in enterprise search, the exact query terms appeared in relevant documents only 23% of the time. The remaining 77% required understanding synonyms, related concepts, or paraphrased ideas.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A core limitation of keyword search is vocabulary mismatch: users and document authors often describe the same concept with different words.
claims.rationale: This named study and its exact percentages are not backed by a verifiable primary source in the module.

claims.claim_text: The module says embedding-based search typically outperforms keyword search by 30-60% on relevance metrics.
claims.span_hint: section: Why Embeddings Beat Keyword Search / closing sentence
claims.anchor_text: This semantic understanding is why embedding-based search typically outperforms keyword search by 30-60% on relevance metrics.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Embedding-based retrieval often improves recall for synonym-heavy or paraphrased queries, but the size of the gain depends on the dataset and evaluation setup.
claims.rationale: The precise performance range is benchmark-dependent and unsourced here.

claims.claim_text: The module says MTEB was released in 2022 and ranks embedding models on 56 diverse tasks.
claims.span_hint: section: The Benchmark Wars / opening sentence
claims.anchor_text: In **2022**, the MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark) was released, ranking embedding models on 56 diverse tasks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In 2022, the MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark) was introduced as a broad evaluation suite for text-embedding models.
claims.rationale: The benchmark is real, but the specific '56 tasks' wording does not match the primary source and should be corrected rather than force-cited.

claims.claim_text: The module says open-source embedding models are within 2-3% of commercial models.
claims.span_hint: section: The Benchmark Wars / current state bullet 3
claims.anchor_text: - Open source is now within 2-3% of commercial models!
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Open-source embedding models have narrowed the gap with commercial offerings on several benchmarks.
claims.rationale: The exact percentage gap varies by benchmark, language, and task mix.

claims.claim_text: The module says Nils Reimers created Sentence-BERT during his PhD and that the paper was rejected from NeurIPS before being accepted to EMNLP.
claims.span_hint: section: The Billion-Dollar Pivot: From Words to Sentences / 2019 paragraph
claims.anchor_text: **2019**: Nils Reimers (a German researcher) created **Sentence-BERT** while doing his PhD. His insight: train BERT to produce embeddings that are fast to compare. The paper was rejected from the main NeurIPS conference but accepted to EMNLP.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In 2019, Nils Reimers and Iryna Gurevych introduced Sentence-BERT, which adapted BERT into a more practical sentence-embedding approach.
claims.rationale: The historical publication anecdote about rejection and venue trajectory is not backed here by a primary source.

claims.claim_text: The module says Sentence-BERT downloads exceed 100M per month on Hugging Face.
claims.span_hint: section: The Billion-Dollar Pivot: From Words to Sentences / impact bullet 4
claims.anchor_text: - Downloads: 100M+ per month on Hugging Face
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Sentence-transformer models are widely used across the NLP ecosystem.
claims.rationale: This is a dynamic platform statistic without a cited source.

claims.claim_text: The module says the famous king/queen analogy was discovered by accident during debugging.
claims.span_hint: section: The Google Intern Who Changed NLP Forever / fun fact
claims.anchor_text: **Fun fact**: The famous king/queen example was discovered by accident when a researcher was debugging the model and tried random arithmetic operations!
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Fun fact**: The king/queen analogy became one of the most famous demonstrations of vector arithmetic in word embeddings.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced discovery anecdote presented as fact.

further_reading.title: Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space
further_reading.why_relevant: The original 2013 Word2Vec paper anchors the historical beginning of efficient neural word-vector learning.

further_reading.title: Distributed Representations of Words and Phrases and their Compositionality
further_reading.why_relevant: This paper covers the analogy-style compositional behavior that made embeddings famous.

further_reading.title: Sentence-BERT: Sentence Embeddings using Siamese BERT-Networks
further_reading.why_relevant: It is the canonical paper for practical sentence embeddings and semantic similarity with BERT-derived encoders.

further_reading.title: OpenAI Embeddings Guide
further_reading.why_relevant: It documents current OpenAI embedding dimensions, max input limits, and common embedding use cases.

further_reading.title: MTEB: Massive Text Embedding Benchmark
further_reading.why_relevant: It provides the benchmark context needed for discussing how embedding models are evaluated across tasks.

notes: Excluded code blocks, quizzes, and pure teaching analogies. Seeded the hard-claim surface: historical anecdotes, vendor/model specs, market/pricing claims, benchmark references, and all quantified war-story bullets that would otherwise remain unsourced.
